name: Capability Types

laws: Authority is conveyed only by holding a capability value
No ambient authority: code cannot act without a capability in scope
Capabilities are unforgeable and explicitly passed
x.encoding: Unforgeable tokens / handles whose type grants a specific permission; passed explicitly. Approximated by sealed handle types, effect/permission markers, or Rust ownership of resource handles.

def: A discipline where the right to perform an operation is represented by a typed, unforgeable value, so code can only do what it has been explicitly handed the capability for.
problem: Ambient authority (any code can open files, network, etc.) makes least-privilege hard to enforce and reason about.
mechanism: Permissions become first-class typed values that must be passed to the code that uses them. Without the capability in scope, the operation is not expressible — turning authorisation into a static, compositional property.
benefits: Authority is explicit and auditable in signatures
No ambient global authority to abuse
limits: Retrofitting onto ambient-authority APIs is hard
failures: Smuggling authority via globals, defeating the model
whenNot: Small programs where ambient authority is acceptable
Ecosystems built entirely on ambient APIs
myths: "ACLs and capabilities are the same" — capabilities bind authority to a held value, not to identity checks at the door
